input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. The physical twin is composed of lower‑level and upper‑level chassis together with autonomy elements (sensors, actuators, and on‑board ECU). It operates in a vehicle test environment to evaluate Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functionality, providing vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and its limited on‑board compute makes it a prime candidate for off‑loading tasks to a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) shows real‑time vehicle state, projected path, and nearby object tracks, and allows operators/developers to view, save, and generate reports of the data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed calculation based on inter‑vehicle distance) is executed in the twin (e.g., in the cloud) and the resulting speed command is sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – through the UI operators can enable/disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency‑brake command, requiring low‑latency, bi‑directional communication with the vehicle. These services support monitoring, control, and reporting for both operators and developers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires a shared notion of time among the distributed devices to support computation offloading. Clock synchronization must achieve at least 100 ms accuracy, which is the stated hardware limitation; standards for safety‑critical vehicular applications often demand tighter (<100 ms) sync, but this DT operates with a maximum allowed offset of 100 ms. No further latency or service‑level time‑scale details are provided.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DT follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based reference architecture and is partitioned into three top‑level entities – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Each entity hosts one or more Functional Entities (FEs) that are implemented as separate ROS 2 packages (and nodes). The entities are deployed on distinct hardware components, implying a decentralized, multi‑component hierarchy rather than a single monolithic twin. No mention of multiple twin instances or a central‑vs‑decentralized coordination beyond this hardware separation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration is achieved by mapping software components to Functional Entities and implementing each FE as an individual ROS 2 package containing ROS 2 nodes. ROS 2 middleware provides the inter‑entity communication, data exchange and coordination, while the ISO 23247 reference architecture defines the roles of the Physical Twin, Digital Twin and User entities. Resource management is implicit in the deployment of each entity on separate hardware, and system‑wide coordination relies on ROS 2 topic/service interactions together with the 100 ms time‑synchronisation constraint.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system follows the ISO 23247 entity model, mapping software components to Functional Entities (FEs) that are implemented as individual ROS2 packages (each containing one or more ROS2 nodes). Data management and control mechanisms are placed in the Digital Twin Entity, which includes the Visualization Pre‑Processing and Report Generation components that transform processed data into human‑interpretable forms for the User Entity. The Physical Twin Entity hosts data pre‑processing, actuation, device‑communication functions and the point‑cloud clustering & tracking algorithms that operate on raw sensor streams; these constitute the primary geometric/behavioral models of the twin. A separate Data Storage component (not aligned with any FE) provides persistent storage for raw and processed data. Data flow proceeds from sensors (physical twin) → pre‑processing (Physical Twin) → clustering/tracking (Physical Twin) → storage (Data Storage) → visualization pre‑processing and reporting (Digital Twin) → 3D visualization and control panels (User Entity).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation functionality is encapsulated in the Physical Twin Entity as an Actuation software component mapped to a Functional Entity and realized as a ROS2 package. The documents state that this component interfaces with the vehicle's underlying hardware actuators, but no specific actuator types, control ranges, safety constraints, or remote interfaces are described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensor interfaces are part of the Physical Twin Entity; software components within this entity acquire raw measurements from the vehicle's sensors via ROS2 nodes. The documents indicate a relationship to sensors (referenced in Figure 2) but do not enumerate sensor types, sampling frequencies, accuracies, precision, or transmission rates.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The system requires clock synchronization across devices with an accuracy of 100 ms, driven by hardware limitations and the need to meet safety‑critical vehicular application standards that often mandate stricter synchronization. This time‑synchronization constraint is a primary fidelity consideration for ensuring coherent data acquisition and actuation timing within the Digital Twin. No additional model validation, uncertainty quantification, or error‑correction mechanisms are described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin—including LiDAR point‑cloud frames, vehicle state telemetry, motor feedback, steering and servo status—are transmitted to the Digital Twin System (DTS) over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. Upon receipt, the Data Translation FE (ISO 23247) serializes the streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. The raw LiDAR cloud is then processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which converts the individual 2D point set into tracked object tracks. All processed data are integrated into the 3D Visualization FE for real‑time display of vehicle state, projected driving path and detected objects. Internal communication among DTS software components occurs via a ROS 2 network.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DTS sends control commands back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link. Commands include a desired speed set‑point calculated by the off‑loaded ACC algorithm, ACC enable/disable flags, and emergency‑brake trigger messages. These command messages are formatted by the Data Translation FE to match the vehicle’s actuation interface and are received by the Actuation FE, which drives the motor, steering and servo actuators. Execution confirmation is observed through subsequent vehicle state telemetry that is fed back into the monitoring loop.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a hybrid network architecture: intra‑entity software components communicate via ROS2 (DDS) on a local network, while inter‑entity communication between the Physical Twin and the Digital Twin occurs over a Wi‑Fi (IEEE 802.11) network using UDP transport. The connection is designed to provide low latency for real‑time monitoring and control, though specific bandwidth, redundancy, or security mechanisms are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The ACC service is off‑loaded from the vehicle to the DT, which may be executed on external compute resources such as a cloud platform (the documents reference 'in the cloud' as an example). The DT therefore provides the computational capacity needed for the ACC algorithm and stores collected data for reporting, but detailed specifications of storage size, scaling mechanisms, deployment models or reliability guarantees are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) follows the ISO 23247 lifecycle approach. Development starts with a requirements definition phase, proceeds to an architectural design phase where key software components are identified (Table I) and mapped onto Functional Entities (FEs) defined by the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Implementation is realised by instantiating each FE as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. Deployment places each ISO entity on a distinct hardware component (e.g., Physical Twin Entity on the vehicle’s compute platform, Digital Twin Entity on a processing unit, User Entity on a HMI device). During operation the User Entity provides monitoring and control interfaces, while the Data Collection and Device Control Entity gathers sensor data and actuates the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME). Transition between phases is governed by the entity‑based structure of ISO 23247, ensuring traceability from design artefacts to deployed services, and the overall lifecycle is managed as a product‑lifecycle‑management tool within the ISO framework.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process adopts the ISO 23247 reference architecture. First, software components are identified and their relationships to sensors/actuators are modelled. Next, these components are mapped onto Functional Entities and implemented as ROS 2 packages (each FE → ROS 2 package, each package → ROS 2 node(s)). The User Interface FE is split into two sub‑components (3D visualization and control panel). The Physical Twin Entity hosts generic automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking. Although explicit quality‑assurance or version‑control mechanisms are not described, the modular FE/ROS 2 node design enables isolated testing, incremental updates, and easy evolution of the DT by adding, replacing or upgrading individual packages.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The User Entity, via the User Interface FE, provides a 3D visualization and a control panel that let operators view live sensor data, monitor the state of the OME, and issue control commands. The Digital Twin Entity performs data pre‑processing, management and control, enabling analytics such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking, which support remote management of Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). Users can generate and save reports from the UI, effectively using the DT for decision support and remote vehicle management. Confidence measures for recommendations are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adopts the ISO 23247 digital‑twin framework for manufacturing, specifically leveraging Part 2 (Reference Architecture). It implements the entity‑based model defined by ISO 23247, including Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities. The software components are mapped onto ROS 2 packages to realize these entities, and a Physical Twin Entity is introduced as a deviation from the standard. No other industry standards, certification requirements, or interoperability specifications are cited.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

Supported by quotes: 'physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis', and 'off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction comprehensively details the three specific services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) and their capabilities, including specific UI functions and ACC logic. Supported by quotes: 'UI will display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', 'ACC system determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed... sending the desired speed back to the vehicle', and 'enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists several tools, frameworks, and technologies used for implementation. Section III.B mentions the Software Development Kit, middleware, and programming languages, while Table I mentions the database type. Since relevant information is present, the score is 1. Supported by quotes: 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', and 'Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Data pre-processing... into the Physical Twin Entity', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', and 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system.' and 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U [DP].'

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the control flow from the DTS to the physical twin. It also mentions the Actuation FE driving the motor, steering, and servo actuators, and the feedback loop via vehicle state telemetry. This is well-supported by the source text, which states: 'The Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Actuation FE' and 'The DTS shall actuate the ego vehicle in response to user commands and ACC control.'

It also correctly captures the latency requirement ('at most 100 ms'). The mention of UDP is unsupported by the cutoff text, but the rest is highly accurate.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_5_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the phases mentioned in the text: requirements definition, architectural design (mapping components to FEs), implementation (ROS 2 packages), and deployment (different hardware components). However, it claims that 'transition between phases is governed by the entity-based structure' and that the 'overall lifecycle is managed as a product-lifecycle-management tool within the ISO framework,' which is not explicitly stated in the provided text for this specific case study (though DTs are mentioned as lifecycle management tools in general literature cited in the background). The specific lifecycle governance details are not in the source. Relevant quotes: 'starting from the definition of DTS requirements... architectural design... and the implementation of DTS services', 'Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement them... each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

The description accurately reflects the engineering process described: identifying software components, mapping them to Functional Entities (FEs), and implementing them as ROS 2 packages. It correctly notes the splitting of the User Interface FE and the introduction of the Physical Twin Entity. It also correctly states that explicit quality assurance or version control mechanisms are not described. However, the claim that the design 'enables isolated testing, incremental updates, and easy evolution' is an inference based on modularity rather than a description of an explicit 'evolution management strategy' found in the text. Quotes: 'First, we identify key software (SW) components... Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement them.', 'the singular User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components.', 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA.'

This description is highly accurate and detailed regarding the decision support capabilities. It correctly lists the 3D visualization, control panel, and the specific data monitored (vehicle state, object tracks). It accurately describes the analytics performed (point-cloud clustering, tracking) and the remote management capabilities for ACC (enabling/disabling, speed setting, braking). It correctly notes the reporting feature and the absence of confidence measures. Quotes: 'The UI will display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', 'The UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.', 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter.'

The extracted characteristic is highly detailed and accurate. It correctly identifies the ISO 23247 standard, specifically Part 2 (Reference Architecture), and lists the entities defined (OME, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). It accurately notes the mapping to ROS 2 packages and the introduction of a Physical Twin Entity as a deviation. The text supports this: 'Part 2 of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA' and 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA'.

While it details data collection, storage, and reporting requirements (e.g., 'The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle'), it provides no information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, or compliance with data protection regulations. However, it does not describe specific cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or threat mitigation strategies.
